title: Furik and Rubik and Sub Array

content.problemStatement:
Furik and Rubik are playing the game **Sub array**. This game is played over an **array of integers**, consisting of **N** elements.


In this game, Furik decides an integer that is equal to **sum** of some **sub-array** of this array and Rubik wants to guess this number.


But, since Rubik is a busy man, he wants to know how many times in the worst case he will have to try to guess this number. You may assume he acts optimally, i.e. he doesn't call the same number twice, and calls only those numbers, that might have been decided by Furik, meaning, the sum of some or the other **sub-array**.


Let's call the array **b**, sub-array of the array **a**, if there are numbers **l** and **r** such, that **b1 = al AND b2 = al+1 AND ... b|b| = ar**, where **|b|** is the length of the array **b**.
constraints: N·S ≤ 10^10 (S is the sum of the array)
All sums fit into 64‑bit signed integer
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- a line with integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000)
- a line with N positive integers a1 … aN (1 ≤ ai, and N·S ≤ 10^10 where S is the sum of the array)
All numbers are separated by single spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of distinct sums of all possible sub‑arrays of the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation: All sub‑array sums are {1,2,3,6}. There are 4 distinct values, so Rubik may need at most 4 guesses.
The 28 distinct sums listed in the statement are the only possible sums of sub‑arrays.

hidden.label: edge case with many equal elements

solution.approach: For each test case we compute all sub‑array sums. Using prefix sums we can obtain the sum of sub‑array [l, r] as pref[r]‑pref[l‑1] in O(1). We iterate over all pairs (l, r) with l ≤ r (O(N^2) pairs) and insert each sum into a hash set (or a sorted array later). The size of the set after processing the whole array is the answer. With N ≤ 1000 the O(N^2) algorithm is easily fast enough (≈10^6 operations per test case).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·N^2) – each test case enumerates all O(N^2) sub‑arrays.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) in the worst case for the set of distinct sums (at most N·(N+1)/2 elements).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        seen = set()
        for i in range(N):
            cur = 0
            for j in range(i, N):
                cur += arr[j]
                seen.add(cur)
        out_lines.append(str(len(seen)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
